content: Melanoma is the most serious skin cancer, arising from melanocytes. Risk factors: UV radiation exposure (intermittent intense exposure, blistering sunburns), fair skin, numerous nevi (>50), atypical nevi, family history, personal history of melanoma, immunosuppression. Genetic predisposition: CDKN2A mutations (familial atypical multiple mole melanoma syndrome), CDK4, BAP1. Molecular features: BRAF V600 mutations (~50%, V600E most common); NRAS (~20%); NF1 (~15%); triple wild-type (~15%); KIT mutations in acral/mucosal melanoma. Subtypes: superficial spreading (most common, radial growth phase); nodular (vertical growth, aggressive); lentigo maligna (chronic sun-damaged skin, elderly); acral lentiginous (palms, soles, nail beds, not UV-related, more common in darker skin); mucosal (rare, poor prognosis); uveal/ocular (distinct biology, GNAQ/GNA11 mutations). Breslow thickness (mm) is the most important prognostic factor for localized disease.
